items.myth: Gradient boosting and random forest are the same because both use many trees.
items.reality: Random forests bag independent deep trees and average them to reduce variance. Gradient boosting trains shallow trees sequentially, each fitting pseudo-residuals from the current ensemble to reduce bias step by step.
items.example: A forest with 500 parallel trees differs from 500 boosting rounds where tree 200 only exists to fix errors left after trees 1–199.

items.myth: Boosting always fits the raw label y on every tree.
items.reality: After initialization, each tree fits pseudo-residuals (negative loss gradient), not y directly. For MSE those residuals equal y − F(x), but for log loss they are a different corrective target.
items.example: Round 3's tree learns to predict (y − F₂(x)), not to re-predict y from scratch.

items.myth: More trees always improve the model without limit.
items.reality: Without shrinkage or early stopping, adding trees eventually overfits training residuals. Validation error often forms a U-shape or plateaus; stop when validation loss stops improving.
items.example: Training MSE may keep falling after round 400 while validation MSE rises—signal to reduce n_estimators or lower η.
